summary: Stancil argues that the data industry’s core pain isn’t tooling or process but the need to work on layered caches; unlike software, data pipelines rarely run live end-to-end. If instant, on-demand processing were possible, reliability, debugging, and trust would improve dramatically, but until then, copying software engineering practices only helps at the margins.
money_quotes: In data development, people are working off of an infinitely splintering collection of caches.
Every number or chart we see is a cache on a cache on a cache.
The entire job is practically cache management.
If everything could be updated immediately, most of these problems would go away.
Until we have our superconductor, we can’t actually work like engineers.
key_insight: The data stack’s single point of failure is its reliance on cached, stepwise processing, and only near-instant end-to-end computation would make data work as reliable as software engineering.
